title: Symplectic Topology
speaker: Lev Buhovsky
abstract: The Arnold conjecture about fixed points of Hamiltonian diffeomorphisms was partly motivated by the celebrated Poincare-Birkhoff fixed point theorem for an area-preserving homeomorphism of an annulus in the plane. Despite the fact that the Arnold conjecture was formulated in he smooth setting, several attempts to return to the continuous setting of homeomorphisms and to study the conjecture in this setting has been made afterwards. In this talk I will describe some old and more recent results on the subject. Based on a joint work with V. Humiliere and S.

proof_stages.label: C0 Arnold problem and failure of fixed-point counts
proof_stages.summary: The speaker formulates the degenerate Arnold conjecture, defines Hamiltonian homeomorphisms as C0 limits of Hamiltonian diffeomorphisms, reviews the surface theorem, and uses the higher-dimensional one-fixed-point result to motivate replacing fixed-point counts by action-spectral data.

proof_stages.label: Morse spectral invariants as the LS model
proof_stages.summary: For a smooth function, sublevel-set homology and minimax define spectral invariants, and an equality under intersection forces a large critical level, yielding the cup-length lower bound for critical levels.

proof_stages.label: Floer action filtration and time-one-map spectral invariants
proof_stages.summary: In the symplectically aspherical setting, the Hamiltonian action functional and filtered Floer complex reproduce the Morse construction, with Schwarz--Seyfaddini invariance making spectral values functions of the Hamiltonian diffeomorphism up to shift.

proof_stages.label: Floer--Lusternik--Schnirelmann dichotomy
proof_stages.summary: The Morse equality lemma has a Floer analogue: coincident spectral invariants for intersecting homology classes imply a homologically nontrivial fixed-point set at one action, so a diffeomorphism has either at least cup-length-plus-one spectral values or infinitely many fixed points.

proof_stages.label: Extension to Hamiltonian homeomorphisms via C0 continuity
proof_stages.summary: The C0 theorem is made meaningful by defining spectral-invariant differences through the C0-continuous spectral norm, which lets these invariants—and hence Hofer's dichotomy—extend from diffeomorphisms to Hamiltonian homeomorphisms.

proof_stages.label: Barcode refinement
proof_stages.summary: Viewing filtered homology as a persistence module gives a barcode refinement, and gamma-continuity of barcodes allows the whole Floer barcode, up to shift, to be assigned to a Hamiltonian homeomorphism.

proof_stages.label: Surface recovery from action clustering
proof_stages.summary: Returning to fixed points, the speaker explains that in dimension two distinct spectral values should force different fixed points via an approximation lemma whose nearby fixed points over each limiting fixed point have almost equal actions, giving a route to Matsumoto's bounds.

examples.name: Sphere and positive-genus surface bounds
examples.note: The sphere and genus-g surface cases make Matsumoto's C0 Arnold theorem concrete by giving the bounds two and three and identifying the relevant area-preserving homeomorphisms; they illustrate the problem rather than generating the spectral strategy.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker states the sphere and positive-genus surface cap-length lower bounds, but does not derive or check them on the surfaces

passes.reason: The sphere case is the genus-zero, cup-length-one base case, and the quoted fixed-point numbers are lower-bound values rather than adversarial examples.
The sphere is the genus-zero minimal boundary case among closed surfaces, and the stated bounds illustrate the theorem rather than refute a claim.
These are cap-length fixed-point lower bounds for closed surfaces, hence minimum guaranteed counts, cited as a positive theorem.
The example records the sharp low-dimensional surface lower bounds, with the sphere as the genus-zero boundary case among closed surfaces.
The surface cases include the sphere, a maximally symmetric closed surface, and are used as positive Arnold-bound cases rather than to refute a claim.
The surface cases include the genus-zero sphere, a minimal boundary case among closed orientable surfaces, used only to state positive fixed-point lower bounds.
The sphere and positive-genus cases are presented via cap-length fixed-point lower bounds, so the example is extremal as a bound but not adversarial.
The surface bounds are used to state Matsumoto’s two-dimensional case, with the sphere a genus-zero/minimal cap-length boundary case rather than an obstruction.
Matsumoto’s surface cases are cited as affirmative lower-bound cases, not failures; the sphere is the genus-zero base case and the stated fixed-point counts are cup-length lower bounds.
They are the dimension-two cap-length lower-bound cases, giving fixed-point minima 2 for S^2 and 3 for positive genus rather than refuting a claim.
The sphere and positive-genus cases are used as positive surface instances of the C0 Arnold theorem, with the quoted fixed-point counts serving as sharp cap-length lower bounds.
The sphere/genus-positive cases give cap-length fixed-point lower bounds for closed surfaces, used as positive sharp-bound cases rather than as a disproof.

examples.name: Torus height function with green and red cycles
examples.note: The height function on a torus, with two displayed cycles, visualizes the minimax definition by showing their spectral values at different critical levels, so it is a concrete illustration of an already stated construction.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker reasons through the torus height-function picture, pushes the green/red cycles, and identifies their spectral invariants as specific critical levels
